dialogues.title: Asking for opening hours
dialogues.context: At a small shop in Funchal, asking a shop assistant about opening hours.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Bom dia. Que horas abre a loja?
lines.translation: Good morning. What time does the shop open?

lines.speaker: Shop Assistant
lines.text: Bom dia. Abrimos às nove da manhã.
lines.translation: Good morning. We open at nine in the morning.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: E que horas fecha?
lines.translation: And what time does it close?

lines.speaker: Shop Assistant
lines.text: Às sete da noite, de Segunda a Sábado. Domingo, fechamos às duas.
lines.translation: At seven in the evening, from Monday to Saturday. On Sunday, we close at two.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Muito obrigado!
lines.translation: Thank you very much!

dialogues.title: Making plans with a friend
dialogues.context: Two friends chatting about their plans for the week.

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.text: Olá! Que fazes amanhã?
lines.translation: Hi! What are you doing tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Amanhã é Terça-feira, certo? À tarde, vou à praia.
lines.translation: Tomorrow is Tuesday, right? In the afternoon, I'm going to the beach.

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.text: Pois é! E à noite?
lines.translation: That's right! And in the evening?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: À noite, não sei. Talvez fique em casa.
lines.translation: In the evening, I don't know. Maybe I'll stay home.

lines.speaker: Friend
lines.text: Ok. Então, falamos depois. Bom fim de semana!
lines.translation: Okay. So, we'll talk later. Have a good weekend!

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.text: Obrigado! Tu também.
lines.translation: Thanks! You too.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a bus stop in Funchal. You want to ask a local about the next bus to Câmara de Lobos.

nodes.npc_text: Diga?
nodes.npc_translation: Yes?

options.text: Desculpe, que horas é o próximo autocarro para Câmara de Lobos?
options.translation: Excuse me, what time is the next bus to Câmara de Lobos?

nodes.npc_text: O próximo é às dez e meia. Está a ver aquele horário ali?
nodes.npc_translation: The next one is at half past ten. Do you see that timetable over there?

options.feedback: Good, confirming the time and day is useful.
A polite exit, but you could have confirmed the day.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, sim. Hoje. Daqui a um bocado.
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, yes. Today. In a little while.

options.translation: Thank you very much for the help!
options.feedback: Excellent, a polite and clear thank you.
A bit more informal, but still polite.

nodes.npc_text: De nada. Bom dia!
nodes.npc_translation: You're welcome. Good day!

mission.title: Find out when the market opens
prep: Practice asking: 'Bom dia. Que horas abre o mercado?' (Good morning. What time does the market open?)
Be ready to understand times like 'nove e meia' (nine thirty) or 'oito da manhã' (eight in the morning).
Practice thanking them: 'Muito obrigado!'
likely_responses: Bom dia! Abre às sete da manhã.
Abre às oito e meia, de Segunda a Sábado.

variants.translation: It's at one in the afternoon.

cultural_notes.title: Expressing Half-Hours
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, when telling time, 'meia' (half) is commonly used after the hour, like 'dez e meia' (half past ten). It's less common to say 'dez e trinta' (ten thirty) in everyday speech, though it's understood.

cultural_notes.title: Asking for the Time Politely
cultural_notes.body: When asking a stranger for the time, it's polite to start with 'Desculpe' (Excuse me) or 'Com licença' (Excuse me/Pardon me) before 'Que horas são?' (What time is it?). This shows respect and acknowledges you're interrupting them.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Que horas são?"
Say this phrase from memory: "Às oito da manhã."
Say this phrase from memory: "Segunda-feira."
Say this phrase from memory: "Daqui a uma semana."
Recall the Portuguese for: Today
Recall the Portuguese for: Tomorrow
Recall the Portuguese for: Yesterday
Recall the Portuguese for: Half (time)
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

dialogues.title: Buying a Bolo do Caco
dialogues.context: At a small café in Funchal, buying a traditional Madeiran snack.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Olá! Quanto custa um bolo do caco?
lines.translation: Hello! How much is a bolo do caco?

lines.speaker: Café worker
lines.text: É dois euros e cinquenta, o senhor.
lines.translation: It's two euros and fifty, sir.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Dois euros e cinquenta. Ok. Pode pagar com cartão?
lines.translation: Two euros and fifty. Okay. Can I pay with card?

lines.speaker: Café worker
lines.text: Pois, sim. Multibanco.
lines.translation: Yes, of course. Multibanco (card).

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Obrigado!
lines.translation: Thank you!

dialogues.title: Buying a Souvenir
dialogues.context: At a souvenir shop, asking the price of a small item.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Bom dia! Quanto custa este chapéu?
lines.translation: Good morning! How much does this hat cost?

lines.speaker: Shop owner
lines.text: Bom dia! São dezassete euros e noventa e cinco cêntimos.
lines.translation: Good morning! It's seventeen euros and ninety-five cents.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Dezassete euros e noventa e cinco. Entendido. E aceitam dinheiro?
lines.translation: Seventeen euros and ninety-five. Understood. And do you accept cash?

lines.speaker: Shop owner
lines.text: Sim, claro. Dinheiro, ou cartão, tanto faz.
lines.translation: Yes, of course. Cash, or card, either is fine.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Ok, obrigado. Vou pagar com dinheiro.
lines.translation: Okay, thank you. I'll pay with cash.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small market stall in Madeira, buying some fresh fruit. The vendor tells you the price, and you need to confirm it and ask about payment.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! São doze euros e cinquenta cêntimos, por favor.
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! It's twelve euros and fifty cents, please.

options.text: Doze euros e cinquenta. Ok. Pode pagar com cartão?
options.translation: Twelve euros and fifty. Okay. Can I pay with card?
options.feedback: Great job confirming the price and asking about payment!

options.translation: How much does it cost? I didn't understand.
options.feedback: It's good to ask for clarification if you don't understand the number.

nodes.npc_text: São doze euros e cinquenta cêntimos. Doze e cinquenta.
nodes.npc_translation: It's twelve euros and fifty cents. Twelve fifty.

options.text: Ah, ok. Pode pagar com cartão?
options.translation: Ah, okay. Can I pay with card?
options.feedback: Perfect, you got the number and moved on to payment.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, Multibanco. Ou dinheiro, como preferir.
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, Multibanco (card). Or cash, as you prefer.

options.text: Vou pagar com cartão, por favor.
options.feedback: Excellent, you stated your preferred payment method clearly.
Good choice, you specified cash.

nodes.npc_text: Pois, aqui está. Obrigado e volte sempre!
nodes.npc_translation: Okay, here you go. Thank you and come back soon!

options.feedback: Perfect! You completed the transaction politely.

mission.title: Buy a small item at a local shop
prep: Practice saying numbers 11-100 out loud.
Rehearse 'Quanto custa?' and 'Pode pagar com cartão?'
Prepare to confirm the price you hear (e.g., 'Dezasseis euros, ok.').

variants.text: Fica trinta e cinco euros e cinquenta.
variants.translation: It comes to thirty-five euros and fifty (cents).

cultural_notes.title: Multibanco: The Portuguese Payment System
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Multibanco' refers to the national interbank network of ATMs and payment terminals. When you ask 'Pode pagar com cartão?', shopkeepers often reply with 'Multibanco' to confirm that card payments are accepted via this system. It's a very common and efficient way to pay, often preferred over cash for larger amounts.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Quanto custa?"
Say this phrase from memory: "São dezasseis euros."
Say this phrase from memory: "Pode pagar com cartão?"
Say this phrase from memory: "Multibanco."

Recall the Portuguese for: Fourteen (EU)
Recall the Portuguese for: Change
Recall the Portuguese for: Cash

dialogues.title: Morning Activities
dialogues.context: A tourist is at a café, talking to a local about their morning.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Bom dia! O que fizeste hoje de manhã?
lines.translation: Good morning! What did you do this morning?

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Bom dia! Hoje de manhã fui ao mercado.
lines.translation: Good morning! This morning I went to the market.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Ah, que bom! E gostaste?
lines.translation: Oh, that's good! And did you like it?

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Sim, gostei imenso! Depois comi um bolo do caco.
lines.translation: Yes, I liked it a lot! Afterwards I ate a bolo do caco.

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Pois é, é muito bom!
lines.translation: Indeed, it's very good!

roleplay.scenario: You are at a café in Funchal. The person serving you asks about your morning.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! O que fizeste hoje de manhã?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! What did you do this morning?

options.text: Hoje de manhã fui à praia.
options.translation: This morning I went to the beach.
options.feedback: Great start! You used 'fui' correctly.
Perfect! 'Fiz' is the correct past tense for 'to do'.

nodes.npc_text: Que bom! E gostaste da praia?
nodes.npc_translation: How nice! And did you like the beach?

options.translation: Yes, I liked it a lot.
options.feedback: Excellent! 'Gostei muito' is a natural way to express enjoyment.
Well done! 'Imenso' is common in Portugal.

nodes.npc_text: Uma caminhada? Onde fizeste?
nodes.npc_translation: A walk? Where did you do it?

options.feedback: Good job using the local term 'levada'!
You correctly used 'fui' for 'I went'.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, as levadas são lindas! E gostaste?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, the levadas are beautiful! And did you like it?

options.translation: I liked it a lot, yes.
options.feedback: Perfect! Confirmed your enjoyment.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é, fico contente que tenhas gostado. Queres mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Indeed, I'm glad you liked it. Do you want anything else?

mission.title: Share Your Morning
prep: Think about one thing you did this morning using 'Eu fui...', 'Eu fiz...', or 'Eu comi...'.
Practice saying 'Gostei imenso!' or 'Gostei muito!'.

variants.text: Hoje à tarde fui ao mercado.
variants.translation: This afternoon I went to the market.
Yesterday I went to the beach.
In the morning I went downtown.
I ate a bolo do caco.
Afterwards I ate an ice cream.
Yes, I liked it a lot!

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois' and 'Pois é'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Pois' or 'Pois é' are very common interjections used to express agreement, acknowledgement, or to confirm something. They can often be translated as 'Indeed', 'That's right', or simply 'Yes' in context, but carry a slightly more emphatic or reflective tone than a simple 'Sim'.

cultural_notes.title: The Versatility of 'Fazer'
cultural_notes.body: The verb 'fazer' (to do/make) is extremely common. In the past tense ('Eu fiz'), it can be used for a wide range of activities, from 'fazer uma caminhada' (to go for a walk) to 'fazer compras' (to do shopping) or 'fazer exercício' (to exercise).

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Hoje de manhã fui..."
Say this phrase from memory: "Comi um pastel."
Say this phrase from memory: "Fiz uma caminhada."
Say this phrase from memory: "Gostei imenso."
Recall the Portuguese for: I went
Recall the Portuguese for: I did/made
Recall the Portuguese for: I ate
Recall the Portuguese for: I arrived

dialogues.title: A Coffee and a Chat
dialogues.context: At a café counter, describing the coffee and the weather.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Olá! Um café, por favor.
lines.translation: Hello! A coffee, please.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Pois, aqui está. Está bom?
lines.translation: Here you go. Is it good?

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Sim, está muito bom! E está quente. Perfeito.
lines.translation: Yes, it's very good! And it's hot. Perfect.

lines.speaker: Barista
lines.text: Está fresco hoje, não está?
lines.translation: It's cool today, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Sim, está fresco, mas está bom.
lines.translation: Yes, it's cool, but it's good.

dialogues.title: People and Place
dialogues.context: Two friends talking about their impressions of Madeira.

lines.speaker: Friend 1
lines.text: As pessoas aqui são simpáticas, não são?
lines.translation: The people here are friendly, aren't they?

lines.speaker: Friend 2
lines.text: Pois são, são muito simpáticas. E a ilha é bonita.
lines.translation: Yes, they are, they're very friendly. And the island is beautiful.

lines.speaker: Friend 1
lines.text: Sim, é linda! E o tempo está bom.
lines.translation: Yes, it's beautiful! And the weather is good.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small café in Funchal. The waiter comes to check on you.

nodes.npc_text: Está tudo bem? O café é bom?
nodes.npc_translation: Is everything okay? Is the coffee good?

options.feedback: A simple and polite way to confirm satisfaction.
Good, you specified what is good. Now, describe its condition.
A slightly unexpected but relevant observation. Good use of 'ser'.

nodes.npc_text: Pois é! E está quente?
nodes.npc_translation: That's right! And is it hot?

options.feedback: Excellent, confirming the temporary state with 'estar'.
Good, you used 'estar' correctly for the temperature.

nodes.npc_text: Pois são! Os madeirenses são muito simpáticos.
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, they are! Madeirans are very friendly.

options.feedback: A simple and natural way to agree.

nodes.npc_text: Ótimo! Bom apetite.
nodes.npc_translation: Great! Enjoy your meal.

nodes.npc_text: Que bom! Precisa de mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: That's good! Do you need anything else?

nodes.npc_text: É um prazer tê-lo/a aqui.
nodes.npc_translation: It's a pleasure to have you here.

mission.title: Describe Your Surroundings
prep: Look around you and identify 2-3 things (e.g., a drink, the weather, a person).
Think about one permanent quality (ser) and one temporary state (estar) for each.
Practice saying sentences like 'O café é bom.' and 'Está fresco.'

variants.text: O bolo do caco é barato.
variants.translation: The bolo do caco is cheap.

cultural_notes.title: Ser vs. Estar: More Than Permanent vs. Temporary
cultural_notes.body: While 'ser' often implies permanence and 'estar' implies temporary states, the distinction is more nuanced. For adjectives like 'bom' (good) or 'mau' (bad), 'ser' describes an inherent quality ('O bolo do caco é bom' - The bolo do caco is good by nature/recipe), while 'estar' describes a condition ('O bolo do caco está bom' - The bolo do caco is good right now, e.g., fresh, warm, or tastes good at this moment). For weather, 'estar' is always used: 'Está fresco' (It's cool).

cultural_notes.title: Expressing Agreement: 'Pois é' and 'Sim, é verdade'
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira and Portugal, it's very common to use 'Pois é' (pronounced 'poish eh') or 'Sim, é verdade' ('Yes, it's true') to agree with someone's observation. 'Pois é' is particularly frequent and can also express a sense of 'indeed' or 'that's right,' often with a slight pause or nod. These are natural and friendly ways to participate in a conversation.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "O café é bom."
Say this phrase from memory: "O café está quente."
Say this phrase from memory: "As pessoas são simpáticas."
Say this phrase from memory: "Está fresco."

Recall the Portuguese for: Fresh / Cool
Recall the Portuguese for: Hot

dialogues.title: Morning Routine Chat
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Carlos, are chatting about their morning routines over coffee.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Olá, Carlos! Então, como é a tua manhã, normalmente?
lines.translation: Hi, Carlos! So, what's your morning like, normally?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Olá, Ana! Pois, normalmente levanto-me às sete.
lines.translation: Hi, Ana! Well, normally I get up at seven.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Ah, às sete. E depois? Tomo o pequeno-almoço cedo.
lines.translation: Ah, at seven. And then? I have breakfast early.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Primeiro, tomo o pequeno-almoço. Depois, faço uma caminhada.
lines.translation: First, I have breakfast. Then, I go for a walk.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Que bom! Sempre uma caminhada?
lines.translation: That's good! Always a walk?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Sim, sempre! Deito-me às dez, para me levantar cedo.
lines.translation: Yes, always! I go to bed at ten, to get up early.

dialogues.title: Asking about a friend's day
dialogues.context: Two friends, Sofia and Tiago, are catching up on their daily routines.

lines.speaker: Sofia
lines.text: Olá, Tiago! Como é o teu dia, normalmente?
lines.translation: Hi, Tiago! What's your day like, normally?

lines.speaker: Tiago
lines.text: Olá, Sofia! Pois, é sempre um bocado ocupado.
lines.translation: Hi, Sofia! Well, it's always a bit busy.

lines.speaker: Sofia
lines.text: Ah, sim? E a que horas te levantas?
lines.translation: Oh, really? And what time do you get up?

lines.speaker: Tiago
lines.text: Levanto-me às oito, normalmente. Depois, tomo o pequeno-almoço.
lines.translation: I get up at eight, normally. Then, I have breakfast.

lines.speaker: Sofia
lines.text: E à noite? A que horas te deitas?
lines.translation: And at night? What time do you go to bed?

lines.speaker: Tiago
lines.text: Deito-me às onze, quase sempre.
lines.translation: I go to bed at eleven, almost always.

roleplay.scenario: A new acquaintance at a café in Funchal asks you about your daily routine.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Como é o seu dia, normalmente?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! What is your day like, normally?

options.text: Levanto-me às sete. Depois, tomo o pequeno-almoço.
options.translation: I get up at seven. Then, I have breakfast.
options.feedback: Good start! You used 'Levanto-me' and 'Tomo o pequeno-almoço' correctly.

options.text: Normalmente, faço uma caminhada de manhã.
options.translation: Normally, I go for a walk in the morning.
options.feedback: Excellent! You used 'Normalmente' and 'faço uma caminhada'.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, às sete. E depois do pequeno-almoço?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, at seven. And after breakfast?

options.translation: First, I work. Then, I have lunch.
options.feedback: Well done using 'Primeiro... depois...' to sequence your actions.

options.translation: Normally, I go for a walk.
options.feedback: Great! You used 'Normalmente' and the verb 'faço'.

nodes.npc_text: Que bom! E a que horas se levanta, normalmente?
nodes.npc_translation: That's good! And what time do you get up, normally?

options.feedback: Perfect! Direct and clear answer about your wake-up time.

options.translation: I don't get up very early.
options.feedback: Good use of negation and 'cedo' (early).

nodes.npc_text: Muito bem! E a que horas se deita, normalmente?
nodes.npc_translation: Very good! And what time do you go to bed, normally?

options.translation: I go to bed at eleven.
options.feedback: Excellent! You answered the question about going to bed correctly.

options.translation: I always go to bed late.
options.feedback: Good! You used 'Sempre' and 'deito-me'.

mission.title: Describe your day to a Madeiran friend
prep: Mentally rehearse your morning routine: when you get up, what you do first, then next.
Think about one activity you do 'normally' or 'sempre' (always).
Practice saying when you go to bed using 'Deito-me às...'.
fallback_phrases: Não percebi, pode repetir, por favor?

variants.translation: I always get up at seven.
I get up a bit late.

variants.text: Tomo o jantar com a família.
variants.translation: I have dinner with the family.

variants.text: Primeiro levanto-me, depois tomo o pequeno-almoço.
variants.translation: First I get up, then I have breakfast.

variants.text: Primeiro faço uma caminhada, depois almoço.

First I work, then I go to bed.

variants.text: Normalmente, faço uma caminhada de manhã.
variants.translation: Normally, I go for a walk in the morning.
Normally, I go to bed late.

cultural_notes.title: The importance of 'pequeno-almoço'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'pequeno-almoço' (breakfast) is a significant meal, often taken at home with family, or at a local 'pastelaria' or 'café'. It's more than just a quick bite; it's a social ritual for many, especially when enjoyed out.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'pois' for agreement
cultural_notes.body: You'll often hear 'pois' or 'pois é' in Portugal. It's a very common interjection used to express agreement, understanding, or to acknowledge what someone has said, similar to 'indeed' or 'that's right' in English. It adds a natural, conversational flow.

dialogues.title: Ordering at a Café
dialogues.context: At a café in Funchal, ordering a drink and a snack.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Bom dia! O que é que deseja?
lines.translation: Good morning! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia. Um café e um bolo do caco, por favor.
lines.translation: Good morning. A coffee and a bolo do caco, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Para comer aqui ou para levar?
lines.translation: To eat here or to take away?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Para comer aqui, obrigado.
lines.translation: To eat here, thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Pois, já vai.
lines.translation: Right, coming up.

dialogues.title: Asking for Directions
dialogues.context: On a street in Funchal, asking a local for directions to the bus stop.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Com licença, onde é a paragem de autocarro?
lines.translation: Excuse me, where is the bus stop?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: A paragem? É logo ali, em frente à igreja.
lines.translation: The stop? It's right there, in front of the church.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Ah, ok. Obrigado!
lines.translation: Ah, okay. Thank you!

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: De nada! Bom dia.
lines.translation: You're welcome! Good day.

roleplay.scenario: You've finished your meal at a restaurant and need to ask for the bill and pay.

nodes.npc_text: Está tudo bem? Quer mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Is everything okay? Do you want anything else?

options.text: Sim, está. A conta, por favor.
options.translation: Yes, it is. The bill, please.
options.feedback: Great, direct and polite!
Perfectly acceptable and common.
Good recovery if you didn't catch what they said.

nodes.npc_text: Perguntei se está tudo bem, ou se quer a conta?
nodes.npc_translation: I asked if everything is okay, or if you want the bill?

options.text: Ah, sim. A conta, por favor.
options.feedback: Good, you got it!
Short and clear.

nodes.npc_text: Aqui está a sua conta.
nodes.npc_translation: Here is your bill.

options.translation: Thank you. Can I pay with card?
options.feedback: Asking about payment method is very useful.

options.translation: Thank you. How much is it?
options.feedback: Good question to confirm the total.

nodes.npc_text: São vinte e cinco euros e cinquenta cêntimos.
nodes.npc_translation: It's twenty-five euros and fifty cents.

options.feedback: You've successfully handled the bill! Now you can pay.

nodes.npc_text: Sim, claro. Pode pagar com cartão.
nodes.npc_translation: Yes, of course. You can pay with card.

mission.title: Café Challenge: Order & Pay
prep: Mentally rehearse ordering a 'bica' (espresso) and a 'bolo do caco' (Madeiran bread).
Practice asking for 'a conta, por favor' (the bill, please).
Prepare to ask 'Posso pagar com cartão?' (Can I pay with card?) or 'Posso pagar em dinheiro?' (Can I pay with cash?).
likely_responses: Para comer aqui ou para levar?

cultural_notes.title: Asking for 'the bill'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, the most common and polite way to ask for the bill is 'A conta, por favor.' (The bill, please.). While 'Pode trazer a conta?' (Can you bring the bill?) is also correct, the shorter version is more frequently used in casual settings like cafés and restaurants.

cultural_notes.title: The 'Bica' vs. 'Café'
cultural_notes.body: While 'café' is understood everywhere, in Lisbon and southern Portugal, a standard espresso is often called a 'bica'. In Madeira and northern Portugal, it's more commonly referred to simply as 'um café'. Both are correct, but using 'um café' is universally safe and natural in Madeira.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Full Week 2 Review"
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

dialogues.title: Neighbourhood Chat
dialogues.context: Two neighbours talking about other people in the street.

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.text: Olá, João! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Hi, João! All good?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Olá, Marta! Tudo, e contigo? Viste a Maria?
lines.translation: Hi, Marta! All good, and with you? Did you see Maria?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.text: Vi sim. Ela foi ao mercado, acho eu.
lines.translation: Yes, I did. She went to the market, I think.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Pois é. E o Pedro? Ele está em casa?
lines.translation: Right. And Pedro? Is he home?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.text: Não, ele foi trabalhar. É canalizador, sabes?
lines.translation: No, he went to work. He's a plumber, you know?

dialogues.title: Asking About Newcomers
dialogues.context: A local asking a shopkeeper about a new family in the area.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia! A nova vizinha, como se chama?
lines.translation: Good morning! The new neighbour, what's her name?

lines.speaker: Lojista
lines.text: Bom dia! Chama-se Sofia. Ela é de Lisboa.
lines.translation: Good morning! Her name is Sofia. She is from Lisbon.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Ah, de Lisboa! E o marido dela? Ele é simpático?
lines.translation: Ah, from Lisbon! And her husband? Is he friendly?

lines.speaker: Lojista
lines.text: Sim, eles são muito simpáticos. Ele fez um bolo para os vizinhos!
lines.translation: Yes, they are very friendly. He made a cake for the neighbours!

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local café in Madeira. The owner, who knows everyone, asks you about some neighbours.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Tudo bem? Diga-me, a senhora Maria, ela foi ao mercado hoje?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! All good? Tell me, Mrs. Maria, did she go to the market today?

options.translation: Yes, she went to the market.
options.feedback: Great, you correctly used 'ela foi ao mercado'!

options.text: Não, ela está em casa, acho eu.
options.translation: No, she is at home, I think.
options.feedback: Good, 'ela está em casa' is correct for 'she is at home'.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, pois é! E o senhor Carlos, o vizinho dela? Ele é da Madeira?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, that's right! And Mr. Carlos, her neighbour? Is he from Madeira?

options.feedback: Perfect! You confirmed his origin.
Excellent, you used 'ele é de Lisboa' correctly.

nodes.npc_text: Ela está em casa? Que estranho! E o marido dela, o senhor Manuel? Ele é simpático?
nodes.npc_translation: She is at home? That's strange! And her husband, Mr. Manuel? Is he friendly?

options.feedback: Well done, you described him positively!

options.text: Não, ele é um bocado calmo.
options.translation: No, he is a bit quiet.
options.feedback: Good, 'um bocado calmo' is a natural way to describe someone.

mission.title: Who's Who in the Neighbourhood?
prep: Think about two people you know (real or imaginary) and their names.
Practice asking 'Como se chama?' and answering 'Chama-se...'
Practice asking 'Ele/Ela é da Madeira?' and answering 'Sim, ele/ela é da Madeira' or 'Não, ele/ela é de...'
Practice describing what they did: 'Ele/Ela foi ao mercado/café' or 'Ele/Ela fez um bolo'.

variants.text: Os meus vizinhos chamam-se Ana e Carlos.
variants.translation: My neighbours are called Ana and Carlos.

cultural_notes.title: Talking About Others
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, "ele" and "ela" are used freely, but often dropped when context is clear. "Foi" can mean both "he/she went" and "he/she was".

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Neighbourhood in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: In Madeira, as in many parts of Portugal, knowing your neighbours (vizinhos) and their daily routines is a common part of social life. People often greet each other, exchange pleasantries, and sometimes share information about local happenings. This creates a strong sense of community, especially in smaller towns and villages.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'o senhor' / 'a senhora' for Politeness
cultural_notes.body: When addressing or referring to adults you don't know well, or those older than you, it's common and polite to use 'o senhor' (Mr.) or 'a senhora' (Mrs./Ms.) before their name or as a standalone form of address. For example, 'A senhora Maria' or 'O senhor João'. This is a sign of respect and is widely used in daily interactions, particularly in shops, cafés, or with public officials.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "O meu vizinho chama-se João."
Say this phrase from memory: "Ele é da Madeira."
Say this phrase from memory: "Eles são muito simpáticos."
Say this phrase from memory: "Ela foi ao mercado."
Recall the Portuguese for: Neighbour
Recall the Portuguese for: Is called
Recall the Portuguese for: He/She went/was
Recall the Portuguese for: He/She did/made
Listen to the dialogue at natural speed and answer: what is being asked for?

dialogues.title: At the Café
dialogues.context: A customer is ordering at a café in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Diga! O que deseja?
lines.translation: Yes! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Tenho sede. Preciso de um sumo de laranja, por favor.
lines.translation: I'm thirsty. I need an orange juice, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Sumo de laranja. Mais alguma coisa?
lines.translation: Orange juice. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Não, obrigada. Só isso.
lines.translation: No, thank you. Just that.

dialogues.title: Feeling unwell
dialogues.context: Two friends are talking on the street in Machico.

lines.speaker: Amiga A
lines.text: Olá! Tudo bem? Não pareces bem.
lines.translation: Hi! All good? You don't look well.

lines.speaker: Amiga B
lines.text: Pois, não muito. Tenho dor de cabeça e um bocado de febre.
lines.translation: Yeah, not really. I have a headache and a bit of a fever.

lines.speaker: Amiga A
lines.text: Ah, pois é. Preciso de ir à farmácia. Queres ir comigo?
lines.translation: Oh, right. I need to go to the pharmacy. Do you want to come with me?

lines.speaker: Amiga B
lines.text: Sim, por favor. Tenho que comprar umas coisas.
lines.translation: Yes, please. I have to buy some things.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a pharmacy in Funchal. The pharmacist asks how they can help you.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Como posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! How can I help?

options.feedback: Good, you stated your symptom clearly.
Correctly expressed having a cold.
A bit vague, but a good start. The pharmacist will ask for more details.

nodes.npc_text: Dor de cabeça? É forte? Tem febre?
nodes.npc_translation: Headache? Is it strong? Do you have a fever?

options.text: Sim, é forte. E tenho um pouco de febre.
options.translation: Yes, it's strong. And I have a little fever.
options.feedback: Excellent, you provided the requested details.

options.text: Não, não é muito forte. Só a dor.
options.translation: No, it's not very strong. Just the pain.
options.feedback: Good, you clarified the intensity.

nodes.npc_text: Constipado/a? Tem tosse ou dor de garganta?
nodes.npc_translation: A cold? Do you have a cough or a sore throat?

options.text: Sim, tenho tosse e dor de garganta.
options.translation: Yes, I have a cough and a sore throat.
options.feedback: Well done, you listed your symptoms clearly.
Good, you specified your main symptom.

nodes.npc_text: O que se passa? Tem alguma dor ou sente-se mal?
nodes.npc_translation: What's wrong? Do you have any pain or feel unwell?

options.feedback: Good, you specified the type of pain.

options.text: Tenho sono e um bocado de frio.
options.translation: I'm sleepy and a bit cold.
options.feedback: You clearly expressed your physical states.

nodes.npc_text: Ok. Tenho este medicamento para a dor de cabeça e febre. É bom.
nodes.npc_translation: Okay. I have this medicine for headache and fever. It's good.

nodes.npc_text: Certo. Para a tosse e garganta, tenho este xarope. Quer levar?
nodes.npc_translation: Right. For the cough and throat, I have this syrup. Do you want to take it?

nodes.npc_text: Percebo. Para isso, o melhor é descansar. Precisa de mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: I understand. For that, it's best to rest. Do you need anything else?

mission.title: Order at a local café
prep: Practice saying 'Tenho fome/sede' (I'm hungry/thirsty).
Practice asking for what you need: 'Preciso de um café / um sumo / um bolo do caco, por favor'.
likely_responses: É para comer/beber aqui ou para levar?

variants.text: Tenho uma dor de cabeça forte.
Preciso de um café, por favor.

cultural_notes.title: TER: Have, Need, Feel
cultural_notes.body: "Estou constipado/a" means you have a cold. It is a very common false friend for English speakers!

cultural_notes.title: Expressing physical states: 'Ter' vs 'Estar com'
cultural_notes.body: In European Portuguese, when expressing physical sensations like hunger, thirst, cold, or pain, we almost always use the verb 'ter' (to have) followed by the noun. For example, 'Tenho fome' (I have hunger = I'm hungry) is standard. Saying 'Estou com fome' (I am with hunger) is more common in Brazilian Portuguese and sounds less natural in Portugal.

cultural_notes.title: 'Ter que' vs 'Ter de' for obligation
cultural_notes.body: Both 'ter que' and 'ter de' are used to express obligation in European Portuguese, meaning 'to have to' or 'must'. They are largely interchangeable in everyday speech, especially in informal contexts. So, 'Tenho que ir' and 'Tenho de ir' both mean 'I have to go'.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Tenho fome."
Say this phrase from memory: "Tenho que ir."
Say this phrase from memory: "Tenho dor de cabeça."
Say this phrase from memory: "Preciso de ajuda."
Recall the Portuguese for: Hunger
